title: Today's AI Brief: The Pope isn’t AGI-pilled
summary: A look at the Pope's stance on AI and OpenAI's election safeguards

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI Brief. Today, we're looking at a couple of interesting developments in the world of artificial intelligence. First, the Pope has weighed in on AI, and it's not quite what you might expect. Then, we'll touch on OpenAI's plans to help safeguard elections in 2026. It's a lot to cover, so let's dive in.

According to a report on The Verge, the Pope's call to arms on AI doesn't mention the superintelligent AI that many experts insist is just around the corner. Now, I'm no expert, but it seems to me that the Pope is focusing on the here and now, rather than speculative futures. His comments are part of a larger conversation about AI's impact on society, and it's interesting to see him approach it from a more practical angle. I mean, who doesn't love a good dose of pragmatism from the Vatican?

OpenAI has announced its plans to help with election information and safeguards in 2026. They're working to help people access accurate information, support cyber defenders, and increase AI transparency. Given the role that AI can play in shaping public discourse, it's good to see companies like OpenAI taking proactive steps to ensure their technology is used responsibly. It's a bit of a relief, to be honest – 2026 elections can't come soon enough, right?

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.

sources.title: DuckDuckGo installs are up 30% as users reject being ‘force-fed’ Google’s AI Search
